title: Boomers, not Gen Z, are the generation cutting back most on alcohol
gist: IWSR survey data across 32,000 people in the 15 largest alcohol markets shows baby boomers, not Gen Z, are driving the steepest declines in drinking rates and volumes, while Gen Z participation is actually rising toward the adult average, undercutting the industry narrative that blames younger generations for weak demand.

themes.theme: Alcohol demand decline is structural and boomer-led, not a Gen Z story

insights.claim: IWSR data shows 71% of baby boomers drank alcohol in the past six months (down 2 percentage points in three years), the lowest rate of any generation, while 74% of legal-age Gen Z drank in the past six months, up from 66% three years ago.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Directly contradicts the sell-side/media narrative that Gen Z sobriety is the main demand headwind for spirits companies; investors modeling alcohol demand recovery should weight boomer moderation as the larger structural driver, not youth abstinence trends.
insights.quote: The narrative that Gen Z is the generation of moderation is now conclusively debunked.

insights.claim: IWSR's president Marten Lodewijks says the moderation trend appears driven by lifestyle choices rather than purely economic pressure, indicating a structural rather than cyclical shift in alcohol demand.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: A 'structural not cyclical' framing implies spirits/beverage companies should not expect a rebound in volumes when macro conditions improve, an important variance from typical recession-driven demand narratives.
insights.quote: The moderation trend increasingly appears to be driven by lifestyle choices, resulting in a structural rather than cyclical change.

insights.claim: Average drinks consumed per occasion fell to 3.9 in the latest IWSR survey, down from 4.4 in 2024 and 2025, with boomers logging the lowest per-occasion volume at 2.6 drinks.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Quantifies the pace of volume decline (roughly 11% drop in drinks-per-occasion in one year), a useful benchmark for modeling beverage alcohol volume trends.
insights.locator: The survey of more than 32,000 people

insights.claim: Shares of major spirits companies including Diageo, Pernod Ricard, and Brown-Forman have languished amid the demand decline, with debate over whether it stems from inflation or a longer-term shift to healthier lifestyles and online socializing.
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Names the specific publicly traded spirits companies whose equity performance is tied to this demand trend, directly actionable for investors in the consumer staples/beverage sector.
insights.locator: Shares at spirits groups such as Diageo

insights.claim: Alcohol consumption is rising in select emerging markets even as it falls globally: among high-income urban earners, drinking participation rose to 77% in India (from 67% three years ago) and 89% in China (from 86%).
insights.speaker: Financial Times
insights.so_what: Identifies India and China's affluent urban cohorts as pockets of growing alcohol demand that could offset developed-market declines for global spirits brands with emerging-market exposure.
insights.locator: While rates are falling globally, in a few emerging markets
